Report all findings with file paths and line numbers.

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 246
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/246](https://www.erdosproblems.com/246)
    23→
    24→Let $a, b \geq 2$ with $\gcd(a, b) = 1$. Then the set of all numbers of the form
    25→$a^k \cdot b^\ell$ (with $k, \ell \geq 0$) is complete: every sufficiently large positive
    26→integer can be written as a sum of distinct elements of this set.
    27→
    28→[Bi59] Birch, B. J., *Note on a problem of Erdős*.
    29→
    30→[Ca60] Cassels, J. W. S.
    31→
    32→[Er61] Erdős, P.
    33→
    34→[He00b] Hegyvári, N.
    35→-/

    37→namespace Erdos246
    38→
    39→/-- The set of all natural numbers of the form $a^k \cdot b^\ell$ for $k, \ell \geq 0$. -/
    40→def twoPowerSet (a b : ℕ) : Set ℕ :=
    41→  {n : ℕ | ∃ k l : ℕ, n = a ^ k * b ^ l}
    42→
    43→/-- A set $S \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is complete if every sufficiently large natural number
    44→can be expressed as a sum of distinct elements of $S$. -/
    45→def IsComplete (S : Set ℕ) : Prop :=
    46→  ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N < n →
    47→    ∃ F : Finset ℕ, (↑F ⊆ S) ∧ ∑ x ∈ F, x = n
    48→
    49→/--
    50→Erdős Problem 246 [Er61] (proved by Birch [Bi59]):
    51→Let $a, b \geq 2$ with $\gcd(a, b) = 1$. Then every sufficiently large positive integer
    52→is the sum of distinct integers of the form $a^k \cdot b^\ell$ with $k, \ell \geq 0$.
    53→
    54→This also follows from a later more general result of Cassels [Ca60] (see Problem 254).
    55→-/
    56→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    57→theorem erdos_246 :
    58→    ∀ a b : ℕ, 2 ≤ a → 2 ≤ b → Nat.Coprime a b →
    59→      IsComplete (twoPowerSet a b) := by
    60→  sorry
    61→
    62→end Erdos246

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 254
    21→
    22→If $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ satisfies $|A \cap [1, 2x]| - |A \cap [1, x]| \to \infty$ and
    23→$\sum_{n \in A} \|n\theta\| = \infty$ for every irrational $\theta$, then every sufficiently large
    24→integer is the sum of distinct elements of $A$.
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/254](https://www.erdosproblems.com/254)
    27→
    28→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_, Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl. (1961),
    29→6, 221-254.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Set Filter BigOperators Classical

    34→namespace Erdos254
    35→
    36→/-- The distance of a real number $x$ from the nearest integer. -/
    37→noncomputable def distNearestInt (x : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    38→  min (Int.fract x) (1 - Int.fract x)
    39→
    40→/--
    41→Erdős Problem #254 [Er61]:
    42→Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be such that
    43→$|A \cap [1, 2x]| - |A \cap [1, x]| \to \infty$ as $x \to \infty$, and
    44→$\sum_{n \in A} \|n\theta\| = \infty$ for every $\theta \in (0,1)$, where $\|x\|$ denotes the
    45→distance of $x$ from the nearest integer. Then every sufficiently large integer is the sum of
    46→distinct elements of $A$.
    47→-/
    48→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    49→theorem erdos_254
    50→    (A : Set ℕ)
    51→    (hgrowth : Tendsto
    52→      (fun x : ℕ => Set.ncard (A ∩ Set.Icc 1 (2 * x)) - Set.ncard (A ∩ Set.Icc 1 x))
    53→      atTop atTop)
    54→    (hsum : ∀ θ : ℝ, 0 < θ → θ < 1 →
    55→      ¬ Summable (fun n : ℕ => if n ∈ A then distNearestInt (θ * (n : ℝ)) else 0)) :
    56→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N₀ ≤ n →
    57→      ∃ S : Finset ℕ, ↑S ⊆ A ∧ ∑ x ∈ S, x = n := by

    59→
    60→end Erdos254

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 351
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/351](https://www.erdosproblems.com/351)
    23→-/
    24→
    25→open Polynomial
    26→
    27→namespace Erdos351
    28→
    29→/-- The set of rational numbers of the form `P(n) + 1 / n` where `n` is a natural number
    30→and `P` is a polynomial with rational coefficients.
    31→
    32→Note: We include `P 0` in there (since `1 / 0 = 0`), but this doesn't change the validity of the
    33→conjecture -/
    34→def imageSet {α : Type*} [Semifield α] (P : α[X]) : Set α :=
    35→  Set.range (fun (n : ℕ) ↦ P.eval ↑n + 1 / n)
    36→
    37→/-- The predicate that a set `A` is strongly complete, i.e. that for every finite set `B`, every sufficiently
    38→large integer is a sum of elements of the set `A \ B`. -/
    39→def IsStronglyComplete {α : Type*} [Semiring α] (A : Set α) : Prop :=
    40→  ∀ B : Finset α,
    41→    ∀ᶠ (m : ℕ) in Filter.atTop,
    42→      ↑m ∈ { ∑ n ∈ X, n | (X : Finset α) (_ : ↑X ⊆ A \ B) }
    43→
    44→/-- The predicate that the rational polynomial `P` has a complete image. -/
    45→def HasCompleteImage (P : ℚ[X]) : Prop := IsStronglyComplete (imageSet P)
    46→
    47→/--
    48→Let $p(x) \in \mathbb{Q}[x]$ be a non-constant rational polynomial with positive leading
    49→coefficient. Is it true that \[A=\{ p(n)+1/n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}\] is strongly complete,
    50→in the sense that, for any finite set $B$,
    51→\[\left\{\sum_{a \in X} a : X \subseteq A \setminus B, X \textrm{ is finite}\right\}\]
    52→contains all sufficiently large integers? -/
    53→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    54→theorem erdos_351 :
    55→    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ P : ℚ[X], 0 < P.natDegree → 0 < P.leadingCoeff → HasCompleteImage P := by
    56→  sorry
    57→
    58→/--
    59→Let $p(x) = x \in \mathbb{Q}[x]$. It has been shown that
    60→\[A=\{ p(n)+1/n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}\]
    61→is strongly complete, in the sense that, for any finite set $B$,
    62→\[\left\{\sum_{a \in X} a : X \subseteq A \setminus B, X \textrm{ is finite}\right\}\]
    63→contains all sufficiently large integers.
    64→-/
    65→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    66→protected theorem erdos_351.variants.X : HasCompleteImage X := by
    67→  sorry
    68→
    69→/-- Let $p(x) = x ^ 2 \in \mathbb{Q}[x]$. It has been shown that
    70→\[A=\{ p(n)+1/n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}\]
    71→is strongly complete, in the sense that, for any finite set $B$,
    72→\[\left\{\sum_{a \in X} a : X \subseteq A \setminus B, X \textrm{ is finite}\right\}\]
    73→contains all sufficiently large integers. -/
    74→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    75→theorem erdos_351.variants.X_sq : HasCompleteImage (X ^ 2) := by
    76→  sorry
    77→
    78→end Erdos351
    79→

    16→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    17→
    18→/-!
    19→# Erdős Problem 123
    20→
    21→*References:*
    22→- [erdosproblems.com/123](https://www.erdosproblems.com/123)
    23→- [ErLe96] Erdős, P. and Lewin, Mordechai, _$d$-complete sequences of integers_. Math. Comp. (1996), 837-840.
    24→- [Er92b] Erdős, Paul, _Some of my favourite problems in various branches of combinatorics_. Matematiche (Catania) (1992), 231-240.
    25→-/
    26→
    27→open Filter
    28→open Submonoid
    29→open scoped Pointwise
    30→
    31→namespace Erdos123
    32→
    33→/--
    34→A set `A` of natural numbers is **d-complete** if every sufficiently large integer
    35→is the sum of distinct elements of `A` such that no element divides another.
    36→
    37→Reference: [ErLe96] Erdős, P. and Lewin, M., _$d$-complete sequences of integers_. Math. Comp. (1996).
    38→-/
    39→def IsDComplete (A : Set ℕ) : Prop :=
    40→  ∀ᶠ n in atTop, ∃ s : Finset ℕ,
    41→    -- The summands come from A
    42→    (s : Set ℕ) ⊆ A ∧
    43→    -- No summand divides another
    44→    IsAntichain (· ∣ ·) (s : Set ℕ) ∧
    45→    -- They sum to n
    46→    s.sum id = n
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Characterizes a "snug" finite set of natural numbers:
    50→all elements are within a multiplicative factor $(1 + ε)$ of the minimum.
    51→Specifically, for a finite set $A$ and $ε > 0$, all $a ∈ A$ satisfy $a < (1 + ε) · min(A)$.
    52→-/
    53→def IsSnug (ε : ℝ) (A : Finset ℕ) : Prop :=
    54→  ∃ hA : A.Nonempty, ∀ a ∈ A, a < (1 + ε) * A.min' hA
    55→
    56→/--
    57→Predicate for pairwise coprimality of three integers.
    58→Requires all three input values to be pairwise coprime to each other.
    59→-/
    60→def PairwiseCoprime (a b c : ℕ) : Prop := Pairwise (Nat.Coprime.onFun ![a, b, c])
    61→
    62→/--
    63→**Erdős Problem #123**
    64→
    65→Let $a, b, c$ be three integers which are pairwise coprime. Is every large integer
    66→the sum of distinct integers of the form $a^k b^l c^m$ ($k, l, m ≥ 0$), none of which
    67→divide any other?
    68→
    69→Equivalently: is the set $\{a^k b^l c^m : k, l, m \geq 0\}$ d-complete?
    70→
    71→Note: For this not to reduce to the two-integer case, we need the integers
    72→to be greater than one and distinct.
    73→-/
    74→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    75→theorem erdos_123 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ a > 1, ∀ b > 1, ∀ c > 1, PairwiseCoprime a b c →
    76→    IsDComplete (↑(powers a) * ↑(powers b) * ↑(powers c)) := by sorry
    77→
    78→/--
    79→Erdős and Lewin proved this conjecture when $a = 3$, $b = 5$, and $c = 7$.
    80→
    81→Reference: [ErLe96] Erdős, P. and Lewin, Mordechai,
    82→_$d$-complete sequences of integers_. Math. Comp. (1996), 837-840.
    83→-/
    84→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    85→theorem erdos_123.variants.erdos_lewin_3_5_7 :
    86→    IsDComplete (↑(powers 3) * ↑(powers 5) * ↑(powers 7)) := by sorry
    87→
    88→/--
    89→A simpler case: the set of numbers of the form $2^k 3^l$ ($k, l ≥ 0$) is d-complete.
    90→
    91→This was initially conjectured by Erdős in 1992, who called it a "nice and difficult"
    92→problem, but it was quickly proven by Jansen and others using a simple inductive argument:
    93→- If $n = 2m$ is even, apply the inductive hypothesis to $m$ and double all summands.
    94→- If $n$ is odd, let $3^k$ be the largest power of $3$ with $3^k ≤ n$, and apply the
    95→  inductive hypothesis to $n - 3^k$ (which is even).
    96→
    97→Reference: [Er92b] Erdős, Paul, _Some of my favourite problems in various branches
    98→of combinatorics_. Matematiche (Catania) (1992), 231-240.
    99→-/
   100→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
   101→theorem erdos_123.variants.powers_2_3 : IsDComplete (↑(powers 2) * ↑(powers 3)) := by sorry
   102→
   103→/--
   104→A stronger conjecture for numbers of the form $2^k 3^l 5^j$.
   105→
   106→For any $ε > 0$, all large integers $n$ can be written as the sum of distinct integers
   107→$b_1 < ... < b_t$ of the form $2^k 3^l 5^j$ where $b_t < (1 + ϵ) b_1$.
   108→-/
   109→@[category research open, AMS 11]
   110→theorem erdos_123.variants.powers_2_3_5_snug :
   111→    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ ε > 0, ∀ᶠ n in atTop,
   112→      ∃ A : Finset ℕ, (A : Set ℕ) ⊆ ↑(powers 2) * ↑(powers 3) * ↑(powers 5) ∧ IsSnug ε A ∧
   113→        ∑ x ∈ A, x = n := by sorry
   114→
   115→end Erdos123
   116→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 869
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/869](https://www.erdosproblems.com/869)
    23→
    24→A question of Erdős and Nathanson.
    25→
    26→[ErNa88] Erdős, P. and Nathanson, M., _Minimal asymptotic bases with prescribed densities_, 1988.
    27→-/
    28→
    29→namespace Erdos869
    30→
    31→/-- The sumset $A + A$: the set of all $a + b$ with $a, b \in A$. -/
    32→def sumset (A : Set ℕ) : Set ℕ := {n : ℕ | ∃ a ∈ A, ∃ b ∈ A, n = a + b}
    33→
    34→/-- A set $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is an additive basis of order $2$ if every sufficiently large
    35→    natural number can be written as $a + b$ with $a, b \in A$. -/
    36→def IsAdditiveBasis2 (A : Set ℕ) : Prop :=
    37→  ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N₀ → n ∈ sumset A
    38→
    39→/-- A set $B \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is a minimal additive basis of order $2$ if it is an additive
    40→    basis of order $2$ and for every element $x \in B$, the set of natural numbers
    41→    not in the sumset of $B \setminus \{x\}$ is infinite. -/
    42→def IsMinimalAdditiveBasis2 (B : Set ℕ) : Prop :=
    43→  IsAdditiveBasis2 B ∧
    44→    ∀ x ∈ B, Set.Infinite {n : ℕ | n ∉ sumset (B \ {x})}
    45→
    46→/--
    47→**Erdős Problem 869** [ErNa88]:
    48→
    49→If $A_1$ and $A_2$ are disjoint additive bases of order $2$, must
    50→$A_1 \cup A_2$ contain a minimal additive basis of order $2$?
    51→-/
    52→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    53→theorem erdos_869 : answer(sorry) ↔
    54→    ∀ (A₁ A₂ : Set ℕ), IsAdditiveBasis2 A₁ → IsAdditiveBasis2 A₂ → Disjoint A₁ A₂ →
    55→    ∃ B : Set ℕ, B ⊆ A₁ ∪ A₂ ∧ IsMinimalAdditiveBasis2 B := by
    56→  sorry

    58→end Erdos869
